narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—which stands above the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset—and your plan to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of applicants. However, our admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated your risk of failure at 44.9%, tipping the scales toward rejection to ensure we select students with the highest predicted success rates. The most influential factor pulling strongly in your favor was your undergraduate GPA, which significantly boosted the model's confidence that you could pass the bar, far outweighing many others. On the flip side, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—below the average of about 5.5—had the next biggest impact, nudging the prediction toward potential failure by signaling room for improvement in later academic performance compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the typical 5.7 average, added a similar push toward higher risk, as did your LSAT score of 31, which falls short of the 36.7 norm and contributed moderately to the concern. Less influential were your upper-middle family income bracket, which slightly raised the risk flag relative to averages, and your full-time status, which offered a small positive lift aligned with most successful students. Overall, while your GPA was a standout that kept the prediction from being even higher risk, the combination of lower deciles and LSAT score compared to typical admitted students drove the final call. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many with similar profiles succeed with targeted prep—but rather a data-driven safeguard for bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to 37 or higher, bolstering your later-year grades through additional coursework, and reapplying next cycle with these enhancements to flip the model's outlook in your favor. You're capable of this, and we're rooting for your success. Keep pushing.
